title: Blood on Upholstery
stainChemistry: Blood contains hemoglobin — an iron-bearing protein that bonds to upholstery through hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions. When fresh, the protein is water-soluble in cold conditions. Heat or time causes the protein chains to cross-link (denature), making the stain insoluble and far harder to remove. The iron component can leave a secondary rust-like discoloration after the protein is removed.
whyThisWorks: Cold water keeps the protein from denaturing and locking into the upholstery fiber. A digestant enzyme (professional) or surfactant (DIY) breaks down the protein chains into soluble fragments that rinse away. The key is treating before any heat exposure — heat permanently cross-links the protein.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Protein Formula (digestant spotter) and tamp gently with spotting brush. Work from edge to center
2. Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and work gently to remove loosened protein and digestant residue. Flush with cold water
3. If stain persists: apply sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent) for stubborn oxidized protein — 5-10 min dwell time, monitor carefully
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse to restore fabric pH
5. Blot dry with clean absorbent cloth. Air dry — no heat
warnings: COLD water only throughout — heat permanently sets protein
Avoid over-wetting — extract excess moisture to prevent backing/foam damage

homeSolutions.instruction: Find the manufacturer's tag (usually in a seam under a cushion). One letter: W = water-safe, S = solvent-only, WS = both okay, X = vacuum only. On S or X, stop and call a pro — water can ring or shrink the fabric worse than blood. On W or WS, continue. No tag? Treat as S and escalate.

homeSolutions.agent: Protein rules: cold only, no alcohol
homeSolutions.instruction: Two hard rules for blood: (1) no hot water — heat denatures hemoglobin and cross-links it into the fiber permanently. Cold at every step. (2) No alcohol-based products (hand sanitizer, rubbing alcohol, alcohol sprays) — alcohol sets protein beyond even professional recovery. If you've already used alcohol, tell the pro in step 9 — it changes their approach.

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test on a hidden section
homeSolutions.instruction: Dab a drop of Dawn + cold water solution on the back of a cushion or the hidden frame. Blot with a white cloth after 60 seconds. If the fabric holds color, proceed. If dye transfers, skip to step 9 — home chemistry will lighten the upholstery dye.

homeSolutions.agent: Blot with a cold damp cloth — don't rub, don't saturate
homeSolutions.instruction: Dip a white cloth in cold water, wring nearly dry, press onto the stain under firm pressure. Lift, re-wet, blot again. Work outside-in. Keep going — fresh sections of cloth each time — until the cloth stops picking up red. Rubbing forces blood deeper into the foam below; blotting only pulls it back out.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn dish soap + cold water — damp cloth only
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one tablespoon of Dawn with two cups of cold water in a bowl. Dip a white cloth, WRING NEARLY DRY, press on the stain. Let sit 5 minutes, then blot with a clean damp cloth. Repeat 2–3 cycles. Dawn + cold water is the home equivalent of a digestant — it emulsifies cell lipids around the protein so cold water can rinse the protein out.

If a pink or brown shadow remains AND the fabric is light (beige, white, cream): dab 3% hydrogen peroxide on a clean white cloth and press onto the stain. Let sit 3–5 minutes, blot. Peroxide lightens hemoglobin pigment. Skip this step on dark or patterned upholstery — the patch test is your safety gate.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with ammonia or bleach. Never exceed 5 minutes dwell on upholstery — longer can lift fiber dye.

homeSolutions.agent: Fan-dry — no hair dryer, no heat
homeSolutions.instruction: Blot until no more moisture transfers to a dry cloth. Point a room fan at the area for 4–8 hours. Good airflow prevents mildew inside the cushion and water rings at the edges. NEVER use a hair dryer or iron — even a brief blast of heat sets any residual protein into the fabric permanently.

homeSolutions.agent: Daylight inspect — repeat if shadow remains
homeSolutions.instruction: Once fully dry, inspect in natural light. Blood often looks gone when damp and returns as a tan shadow when dry. If you see one, repeat steps 5–7 once more. Two rounds is the home ceiling. If a brown halo persists, the protein has cross-linked — escalate.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate to a professional upholstery cleaner
homeSolutions.instruction: If care code is S or X, the patch test failed, alcohol was applied, or the stain survived two home rounds, call a pro. Tell them: "Blood on [W/WS/S/X/unknown] upholstery, treated with Dawn + cold water only + [peroxide if used]. NO hot water, NO alcohol [or: alcohol WAS applied — flag this], damp-cloth only." That handoff lets them jump straight to protein digestants + reducing agents + low-moisture extraction.

steps: 1. Rinse immediately with COLD water. Blot with a clean white cloth — never rub
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup cold water. Apply to stain and blot
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat soap application until stain fades
4. For stubborn stains on whites: apply 3% hydrogen peroxide directly — test colorfastness first, 5 min dwell time
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all soap residue
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer or any heat until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop and take to a professional if the stain has been heat-set, if multiple home treatments haven't improved it, or if the fabric is delicate/expensive
warnings: NEVER use hot water on protein stains — heat sets them permanently
NEVER use alcohol on blood — alcohol permanently sets protein stains
Always test cleaning solution on a hidden area first

neverDo: No over-wetting — causes water rings and backing damage
No chlorine bleach on colored fabric
Always check care code (W, S, WS, X) before treating
fiberSensitivities: Mixed fiber blends common — treat for the most sensitive fiber present
Cushion foam absorbs moisture — avoid saturation
Test in hidden area before treating visible sections

escalation.when: If the protein has been heat-set (hot water, dryer, or iron exposure), or if the stain is older than 48 hours without treatment, or if 3 treatment cycles haven't resolved it
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is blood on upholstery, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the stain fresh and wet, or dried/crusty?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
